title: Memoization
lesson: Cache the results of expensive function calls. If you've solved it before, don't solve it again — a warrior never fights the same battle twice.
lyra_quote: Guardian… I learned that a true warrior remembers every battle. Why solve what's already been solved?

title: Big O Notation
lesson: O(1) is instant. O(n) scales with input. O(n²) slows down fast. Always ask: how does this behave at scale?
lyra_quote: Speed matters in battle. I now measure my strikes not just by strength — but by how they scale against a thousand foes.

title: Recursion
lesson: A function that calls itself. Every recursive problem has a base case (stop!) and a recursive case (keep going). Like a quest within a quest.
lyra_quote: Even quests loop back on themselves… until you find the base of the mountain. That's recursion, Guardian.

title: The Closure
lesson: A function that remembers the scope it was created in, even after that scope is gone. Inner functions carry the memory of their outer world.
lyra_quote: I carry memories of every village I've passed through. Closures do the same — they never forget where they were born.

title: Event Loop
lesson: JavaScript is single-threaded but async. The event loop processes one task at a time from the queue. Callbacks wait their turn — no cutting in line.
lyra_quote: Patience, Guardian. Even I must wait my turn in battle. The event loop ensures no warrior is overwhelmed at once.

title: Promises & Async/Await
lesson: A Promise is a placeholder for a future value. async/await is just clean syntax on top of Promises — same power, more readable.
lyra_quote: I promised to return from this quest. Async/await is just how warriors keep their word — cleanly and without chaos.

title: Hash Maps
lesson: Store key-value pairs with O(1) lookup. Instead of searching through everything, jump directly to what you need. The most powerful data structure for speed.
lyra_quote: Why search a whole forest when you know exactly which tree holds the treasure? Hash maps are a warrior's cheat code.

title: Debounce vs Throttle
lesson: Debounce: wait until the user stops typing. Throttle: fire at most once per time window. Both tame high-frequency events.
lyra_quote: Not every arrow needs to fly the moment the bow is drawn. Debounce waits for stillness. Throttle paces the battle.

title: The Binary Search
lesson: On a sorted array: guess the middle, eliminate half, repeat. O(log n) — incredibly fast. 1 million items? Only 20 guesses needed.
lyra_quote: Split the battlefield in two. Eliminate half. Repeat. That's binary search — and it's how I find enemies in record time.

title: Linked Lists
lesson: Each node holds data and a pointer to the next. Inserts are O(1) but lookups are O(n). Trade speed for flexibility.
lyra_quote: Each step of my journey points to the next. No skipping ahead — but I can always add a new stop without rebuilding the whole path.

title: CSS Grid vs Flexbox
lesson: Flexbox: one-dimensional (row OR column). Grid: two-dimensional (rows AND columns). Use both. Grid for layout, Flex for components.
lyra_quote: Flexbox is a single battle line. Grid is the whole war map. Know when to use each — that's tactical mastery.

title: SQL Joins
lesson: INNER JOIN: only matching rows. LEFT JOIN: all left rows + matches. RIGHT JOIN: opposite. FULL JOIN: everything. Master these and you master data.
lyra_quote: Alliances in battle: some share everything, some share nothing. SQL joins decide who fights together.

title: REST vs GraphQL
lesson: REST: fixed endpoints, multiple calls. GraphQL: one endpoint, ask exactly what you need. GraphQL eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching.
lyra_quote: REST sends the whole army. GraphQL sends only who's needed. Precision over brute force, always.

title: JWT Tokens
lesson: Header.Payload.Signature — a self-contained token. The server doesn't store sessions; the token IS the proof. Stateless and portable.
lyra_quote: I carry my own credentials into every kingdom. No one needs to look me up — my token proves who I am.

title: The Virtual DOM
lesson: React keeps a lightweight copy of the DOM in memory. It compares old vs new (diffing), then only updates what changed. Blazing fast.
lyra_quote: I map the battlefield before the fight. Only charge where things have changed. That's the Virtual DOM — minimal effort, maximum impact.

title: Memento Mori
lesson: Remember that you will die. Not morbid — liberating. It clarifies what matters. The Stoics used it as fuel for living fully, not for fear.
lyra_quote: Every warrior knows the final battle comes. That knowledge? It sharpens every moment before it.

title: Amor Fati
lesson: Love your fate. Not just accept it — love it. Whatever happens, including the hard parts, is exactly what was needed for your growth.
lyra_quote: Every scar from battle is a chapter of my story. I don't just accept what comes — I love it. It made me who I am.

title: The Dichotomy of Control
lesson: Epictetus: some things are in our power (thoughts, actions), some aren't (weather, others' opinions). Focus only on what you control. Obsessing over the rest is suffering.
lyra_quote: I cannot control the storm. Only my blade, my breath, my choices. That's Epictetus — and it's how I stay calm in chaos.

title: First Principles Thinking
lesson: Break problems down to their most fundamental truths. Don't reason by analogy — reason from the ground up. Elon Musk, Aristotle, and Tesla all did this.
lyra_quote: Before I cross the bridge, I ask: why is there a bridge? Do I need a bridge at all? Question everything from the ground up.

title: Wu Wei — Effortless Action
lesson: Taoist concept: act in harmony with the natural flow. Don't force things. The strongest water doesn't push — it flows around. Resistance creates friction.
lyra_quote: The most powerful rivers don't smash through mountains. They find the path. Wu Wei is strength through flow.

title: The Socratic Method
lesson: Ask questions until you reach truth. Challenge assumptions. The wisest people know what they don't know — and that's where knowledge begins.
lyra_quote: I once thought I knew everything about combat. Then a child asked me why I hold my sword that way. Questioning everything is the real weapon.

title: Ikigai — Reason for Being
lesson: The intersection of: what you love, what you're good at, what the world needs, and what you can be paid for. Find that overlap — that's your ikigai.
lyra_quote: My quest only has meaning because it sits where my skill, my passion, the world's need, and my survival all meet. That's ikigai.

title: Nietzsche's Will to Power
lesson: Not power over others — power over yourself. The drive to grow, create, and overcome. Life's fundamental drive is expansion, not comfort.
lyra_quote: Power isn't about conquering others. It's about conquering the version of yourself that wanted to give up.

title: The Ship of Theseus
lesson: If you replace every plank of a ship, is it still the same ship? Are you the same person as 10 years ago? Identity is continuous change.
lyra_quote: My armor has been repaired a hundred times. My skills rebuilt. Am I still the same warrior? The quest says yes.

title: The Beginner's Mind
lesson: Zen concept: approach everything as if for the first time. Expertise can make you rigid — beginner's mind keeps you curious, open, and learning.
lyra_quote: Every forest is new to me, even forests I've crossed a hundred times. That's how I keep seeing paths others miss.

title: The 5-Minute Warrior Flow
lesson: 5 reps of: 10 air squats → 10 push-ups → 30s plank → 5 deep breaths. Resets your nervous system and fires up blood flow to your brain.
lyra_quote: A warrior's body is her first weapon. This 5-minute drill is how I restore my battle-readiness. Try it now, Guardian.

title: Box Breathing
lesson: Inhale 4 counts → Hold 4 → Exhale 4 → Hold 4. Repeat 4 times. Used by Navy SEALs to stay calm under pressure. Takes 90 seconds.
lyra_quote: Before every battle, I breathe like the sea — in, hold, out, hold. It slows the heart and sharpens the mind. Do it now.

title: The Hip Flexor Reset
lesson: Sitting tightens hip flexors, which pulls your lower back. Kneel on one knee, push hips forward gently, hold 30s each side. Instant relief.
lyra_quote: Hours at a desk are like hours in armor — the body stiffens. Free your hips and your whole posture realigns. Do this now.

title: The 20-20-20 Eye Rule
lesson: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Reduces digital eye strain and refocuses your vision after screen time.
lyra_quote: A warrior who strains her eyes in darkness will miss the dawn attack. Rest your vision now — look far, breathe deep.

title: Desk Push-Up Superset
lesson: 10 regular → 10 wide grip → 10 diamond. 30 push-ups total, 3 variations. Hits chest, shoulders, and triceps. 2 minutes flat.
lyra_quote: Your arms are your tools of creation — keep them strong. Three push-up variations, 2 minutes. I'll wait right here.

title: Neck & Shoulder Release
lesson: Slowly: ear to shoulder (10s each), chin to chest (10s), look left/right (10s each). Releases the tension that headaches live in.
lyra_quote: Tension in the shoulders slows the sword arm. Roll them back, drop them down, tilt the head. Release the battle from your neck.

title: Power Pose Reset
lesson: Stand tall, feet shoulder-width, hands on hips, chin up. Hold 2 minutes. Research shows it raises testosterone and lowers cortisol — confidence chemistry.
lyra_quote: Stand as if you've already won. The body shapes the mind. Two minutes of the warrior stance changes your entire chemistry.

title: The Micro Walk
lesson: Walk for 5 minutes — no phone. Walking increases creative thinking by 81% (Stanford study) and flushes stress hormones. Outside if possible.
lyra_quote: Every great warrior walks between battles. No phone, no noise — just movement and sky. Five minutes. Your best idea is waiting outside.

title: Wrist & Hand Reset
lesson: Shake hands loosely (10s), extend wrists back (hold 10s), fist and spread fingers (5x). Prevents RSI — the warrior's work injury.
lyra_quote: Your hands build worlds. Don't let them stiffen into claws. Shake, extend, spread — keep your tools of craft nimble.

title: The Overview Effect
lesson: Astronauts who see Earth from space report a life-changing shift — borders disappear, problems shrink, connection becomes everything. You can trigger this mentally.
lyra_quote: Zoom out far enough and every obstacle becomes a dot on a map. Today's stress? From orbit, it barely registers. Zoom out.

title: The 2-Minute Rule
lesson: If a task takes less than 2 minutes — do it now, immediately. Don't queue it, don't plan it. The overhead of tracking it costs more than doing it.
lyra_quote: Small enemies are best defeated immediately. Don't schedule them — slay them on sight. The 2-minute rule keeps the quest clean.

title: Parkinson's Law
lesson: Work expands to fill the time available. Give yourself 3 hours for a 1-hour task — it takes 3 hours. Set tighter deadlines intentionally.
lyra_quote: A quest with no end date never ends. I always set a sunset deadline. The urgency is the fuel.

title: The Feynman Technique
lesson: Learn anything in 4 steps: Study → Explain it to a child → Find gaps → Review. If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it.
lyra_quote: I teach my technique to the youngest villagers. If they understand, I understand. Complexity is just incomplete understanding.

title: The Starfish Paradox
lesson: You can't save everyone — but you can save one. Do the specific, concrete good in front of you. Scale is not the only measure of meaning.
lyra_quote: I once tried to save an entire kingdom in one battle. I saved none. Then I saved one child — and everything changed. Start small.

title: The 80/20 Rule
lesson: 80% of results come from 20% of causes. Find your 20% — the high-leverage actions — and protect that time fiercely.
lyra_quote: 20% of my training created 80% of my combat skill. I found that 20% and did it obsessively. What's your 20%?

title: The Zeigarnik Effect
lesson: Your brain remembers uncompleted tasks better than completed ones. This is why unfinished work keeps nagging you — and why finishing feels so good.
lyra_quote: Unfinished quests haunt warriors in their sleep. The Zeigarnik Effect — your mind clings to open loops. Close them.

title: Identity-Based Habits
lesson: Don't set goals — set identities. Not 'I want to run a marathon' but 'I am a runner.' Behavior follows self-image. Change who you are, not what you do.
lyra_quote: I am not a warrior because I fight. I fight because I am a warrior. Identity first. The actions follow naturally.

title: Diffuse vs Focused Thinking
lesson: Focused mode: concentrated problem-solving. Diffuse mode: relaxed, background processing — this is when breakthroughs happen. Alternating them is the key to creativity.
lyra_quote: My best battle strategies come during rest, not during battle. The diffuse mind connects what the focused mind misses.

title: The SOLID Principles
lesson: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion. Five principles that keep code clean and extensible as it grows.
lyra_quote: One warrior, one role. Never overload a single fighter. That's SOLID — each piece does one thing, does it perfectly.

title: Wabi-Sabi
lesson: Japanese aesthetic of finding beauty in imperfection and impermanence. Cracked pottery, weathered wood, unfinished work — all beautiful. Perfection is sterile.
lyra_quote: My armor is dented and my cloak is torn. But that's my story, worn visibly. Wabi-sabi — beauty in the imperfect and real.

title: Cold Water Face Trick
lesson: Splash cold water on your face (or wrists) for 30 seconds. Activates the dive reflex — instantly slows heart rate and sharpens mental focus.
lyra_quote: Before my greatest battles I plunge into cold water. It awakens everything. Splash your face now — wake the warrior.

title: The Spotlight Effect
lesson: You think everyone notices your mistakes — they don't. People are too busy thinking about themselves. You are the only one watching you that closely.
lyra_quote: I once stumbled in front of a whole village. Thought they'd never forget. They forgot by sunset. No one watches as closely as you think.

title: Git Rebase vs Merge
lesson: Merge: preserves history, creates merge commits. Rebase: rewrites history, creates a clean linear timeline. Use merge for collaboration, rebase for personal cleanup.
lyra_quote: History can be preserved as it happened, or rewritten cleanly. Merge keeps the truth. Rebase tells a cleaner story. Choose wisely.

title: Kaizen — Continuous Improvement
lesson: 1% better every day. Not revolution — evolution. Compound tiny improvements over time and the results are extraordinary. Japan built industries on this idea.
lyra_quote: I don't try to become the greatest warrior in a day. 1% sharper each morning. That's kaizen — and it's undefeatable.

title: The Sunk Cost Fallacy
lesson: Past investment is irrelevant to future decisions. Don't keep going because you've already spent time or money — decide based on future value only.
lyra_quote: I once kept fighting a losing battle because I'd already fought for hours. A mentor stopped me: 'The past is paid. What does the future cost?' Walk away when needed.
